topic: social media strategy for producer brand

turns.content: okay so I need to figure out my social media game because right now it is NOT giving what it's supposed to give!! like I post clips from the lab and get maybe 40 likes and thats IT. how do producers actually build a following on socials??

That's a really common frustration for producers — the music is great but the content strategy isn't translating to engagement. A few questions first:

1. What platforms are you most active on right now?
2. What kind of content are you posting — finished tracks, behind-the-scenes, process clips?
3. Are you posting on a schedule or just whenever you feel like it?

Instagram mostly and a little bit of TikTok. I post like beat-making clips, sometimes me mixing, sometimes a finished track. NO schedule whatsoever lol I just post when I remember to. which is like once a week maybe??

Okay, that's a solid starting point actually. Instagram and TikTok are the right platforms for music producers. The issue is consistency and content format.

Here's what's working for producers right now:

**TikTok/Reels (short form):**
- "Making a beat from scratch in 60 seconds" — sped-up process videos with the final result at the end
- Before/after comparisons — raw drums vs. mixed drums, dry vocal vs. processed
- "What this sample became" — show the original sample, then your flip
- Reaction-style content where you break down what you did

**Posting frequency:**
- Minimum 3-4 times a week to get any traction with the algorithm
- TikTok especially rewards consistency — posting daily for 30 days can completely change your reach

The key thing: people don't just want to hear the music, they want to see the PROCESS. The messier and more real, the better. Perfect polished clips actually perform worse than raw studio footage.

3-4 times a WEEK?? fam I barely remember to eat sometimes let alone post content lol. but okay I hear you. the process thing makes sense tho because the clips where I'm actually building a beat from nothing do better than when I just post a finished track

Exactly — you already have the data proving it! People want to feel like they're in the studio with you watching it happen. A finished track is just... a track. But watching someone lay down the drums, add the bass, chop a sample — that's a story.

For the consistency problem, here's a hack: **batch create content.** Next time you're in the studio for a full session, set up your phone to screen record or film your hands on the controller. Record EVERYTHING. Then later, chop that footage into 5-8 short clips. One studio session = a week or two of content.

You can also use scheduling tools like Later or Buffer to queue posts in advance. Spend 30 minutes on Sunday scheduling the week and then forget about it.

the batch thing is SMART actually. I always have my phone in the lab anyway. okay but what about captions and hashtags and all that ting?? I never know what to write. I just put like 'new beat!!' and call it a day lol

Ha, "new beat!!" is doing zero work for the algorithm. Here's how to think about captions:

**Captions should do one of three things:**
1. Ask a question — "Would you rap on this or sing on it?"
2. Create debate — "Is this too much bass or not enough?"
3. Tell a micro-story — "Spent 3 hours trying to get this kick right and then accidentally found the perfect sound"

Questions and debates drive comments, and comments are the #1 signal that tells the algorithm to push your content to more people.

**Hashtags:**
- Use 5-8 targeted ones, not 30 generic ones
- Mix broad (#producer #beatmaker) with niche (#dancehallproducer #torontobeats)
- Check what hashtags producers you admire are using

**One more thing:** engage with OTHER producers and artists in your niche. Comment on their posts genuinely, not just "fire emoji." The algorithm notices reciprocal engagement and starts showing your content to similar audiences.

okay wait the question thing in captions is GENIUS because people LOVE giving their opinions on music. like everybody thinks theyre a producer ahlie!! so if I ask them what they think they cant resist commenting

Exactly!! Everyone has an opinion on music and they LOVE sharing it. You can even lean into that — post two versions of the same beat (one with a different bassline or different drums) and ask "A or B?" People will argue in the comments for days.

Another format that gets insane engagement: "Rate this beat 1-10." It's the lowest effort comment someone can make so everyone does it. And every single comment pushes your post higher.

The goal isn't to make everyone a fan — it's to make the algorithm think your content is interesting enough to show to MORE people. Comments and shares matter way more than likes.

A or B polls!! thats so good. okay I'm actually hype about this now. I'm gonna start batch recording in the lab this week and try to get a proper schedule going. like actually commit to it for a month and see what happens

That's the right mindset — commit to 30 days and then evaluate. Don't judge the results after 3 posts. The first two weeks might feel like shouting into the void but around week 3-4 is when consistency starts compounding.

Quick 30-day plan:
- **Week 1-2:** Post daily on TikTok, 3x on Instagram. All process content. Ask a question in every caption.
- **Week 3-4:** Look at your analytics — which clips got the most engagement? Make more of THOSE.
- **Daily:** Spend 10 minutes engaging with other creators in your niche

Also track your follower count and average views at the start so you have a baseline to compare against.

Go build that content library this week and see what happens. Consistency beats perfection every time.
one more ting tho — should I be on YouTube too or is that too much?? I see some producers doing like full beat breakdowns on there

YouTube is great long-term but I'd say don't spread yourself too thin right now. Master TikTok and Instagram first — get that 30-day challenge done. Once you've got a rhythm going on those two, you can repurpose your best-performing short clips into longer YouTube tutorials or breakdowns.

The beauty of starting with short form is that your top-performing clips basically tell you what YouTube content to make. If a 60-second beat flip goes viral on TikTok, turn that into a 10-minute YouTube tutorial. Let the data guide you.
BLESS!! okay I'm literally writing this down rn. 30 day challenge starting this week. the riddim of the content is about to shift fr fr. thank youuu!!

Love it! Just remember — don't let the content creation take away from the actual music-making. The studio work always comes first, the content is just showing people what you're already doing.

Good luck with the 30 days. Come back and tell me how it goes!
